item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually had a line about that in an older version of the skip! Originally there was a much more complicated calculation of how much containment damage the anomaly did — the first time's calculation was a September's worth of days times a set number, the next time's was an October's worth of days, so on and so forth — and whether or not it honoured leap years was actually going to matter, because there'd be one extra day when February rolled around and that would make a difference between near-total failure and total failure.

I didn't include any of that, because it was TOO FUCKING COMPLICATED lol

But yes, to answer the question you actually asked, the anomaly happens on September 8 no matter how many days there are in February. Because, luckily, it's magic and a teeny tiny bit sentient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Important question: does the phenomenon honor leap year?

(I mean, since it takes place on 'the eighth day of September')

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you liked it. I agree that it's very dense and multi-faceted, almost as though the author had only had one very successful SCP so far and wanted to make damn sure that nobody went "ennhhh this is more of a Euclid," so he wildly overcompensated ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don’t usually like SCP’s that are basically ‘maximum threat,’ nor ones that are overly-complex, but I actually quite like this article. Why, I’m not entirely sure, but something about it’s execution just works.

It could probably be a bit less complex, but for the most part, that’s a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welp, now you have to re-upvote it every year or the timeline'll change! Which could be good, actually, depending on how the present one goes.

Thanks! I'm glad it was weird enough for you :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somehow forgot to upvote this. An incredibly over-the-top article — love it.

item.title: Re: SCP-5243: "The Breach that Keeps On Breaching"!
item.content:encoded: Good news! We didn't fuck it up this year.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well! Didn't THIS sucker break away from my pack. Thank you all for voting! I'll know to write something even more bonkers next time.

To celebrate the end of the month since I first posted this… happy new pics day! All my old pics weren't CC-3.0 compliant because I'm a doof. The new ones are, in my opinion, surprisingly similar considering they share no components with the originals. I actually like the top one better now.

If you want to learn more about magic sewer explosions, might I suggest The Significant Others Parts "A" and "B"? I'll throw in a codependent mirror monster and a lovesick janitor if you buy now.

The alternate timelines hinted at here will also become tales eventually. Not soon, but eventually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Only Seven Of You Can Prevent Magic Fires." Thanks :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this has the same feeling as a US Chemical Safety Board accident re-enaction video. i love it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, thanks a ton for all that! The best thing about online print, in my opinion, is that it isn't dead. And I always go back and tweak, so this will be really useful. I'll respond to some of your points individually as I address them, but for now I just want to express my appreciation for your continued education of my baby :P And thanks for the kind words!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this piece's energy, and I love the execution — but I also feel like it could use a couple more proof-reads.

FEEDBACK
– hide block

As SCP-5243 is a recurrent catastrophic containment failure, it is "contained" through amelioration of its effects.

Every year, on the eighth day of September, the following actions must be taken at Site-43:

This is a great opening hook, but I feel like it could be even stronger (and simultaneously more clinical):

The effects of Site-43's recurrent catastrophic containment failure are to be ameliorated at all costs. On September 8th of every calendar year, the following actions must be taken:

Shorter and punchier. But YMMV here, this isn't a strong issue for me; I think the hook is great even as-is. The bit where you end it with the personnel who must perish is also great.

I do feel like you can make this more WTF. Everything you write in this table involves what people must do. But I'd throw in things which objects must do. "The doorway to Containment Cell 24B must evaporate into its component parts", things like that. Anything that makes us think even more "WTF?!".

The "stacking" of these facilities allows waste products from supernatural subjects to proceed vertically from their original sources, to study in Applied Occultism, to neutralization in Acroamatic Abatement.

I love the whole weird industrial vibe you give this, but I'd change 'supernatural' to 'anomalous' — the vibe I think you're aiming for is less "magic" and more "Ghostbusters".

At 18:21 hours, undetected emissions from an anomalous subject in the Security and Containment Section seeped into Acroamatic Abatement Facility AAF-D via Applied Occultism, triggering a catastrophic overload. Orphic outflow reached critical recondicity, and a materials containment breach occurred.1

This is some primo technobabble shit, which is what I think gives the article its great energy. The footnote isn't necessary, but I don't think hurts the piece either — but I'd consider cutting the 'Euclidean geometry' (it's become a bit of a cliche) and maybe replace it with 'terrestrial geometry' or 'local space'. Again, YMMV.

A brief torrent of supernatural activity followed, much of it counter-chronological in nature, with effects including but not limited to:

Again, I'd avoid words like 'supernatural' — you want this to feel like Ghostbusters, I think.

The retroactive and asymmetrical erasure of the above-mentioned entity2

I *love* this bit, but I also think the footnote goes a step too far. I'd dump everything after 'save some cryptic references…'.

He therefore did not receive a telephone call directed to his redline receiver at 18:21 by Janitorial and Maintenance Section technician R. Ambrogi, requesting instructions for the handling of an emergent situation in AAF-D. (Established protocol calls for an immediate activation of the Site-43 Containment Breach Warning System in such a scenario. Technician Ambrogi was either improperly trained, or had forgotten his training.)

Parentheticals like this should be contained within the punctuation of a sentence, not outside of it. IE:

…of an emergent situation in AAF-D (established protocol calls for an immediate…).

If you want a whole set of sentences like this as an aside, you should probably just make it a footnote.

…but was struck by a burst of orphic outflow and biologically inverted.

'biologically inverted' is a great phrase.

At 18:22, fourteen processing tanks ruptured in AAF-D. Dr. H. Blank heard the ruptures in his research laboratory, and remarked upon them. Associate researcher Dr. R. Wirth exited the laboratory in search of the origin of the disruption.

Here's where I'm going to start getting a little critical: I feel like a lot of these sentences are… belabored? They feel like they can be cut down, cleaned up, and made punchier. For instance:

At 18:22, fourteen processing tanks in AFF-D ruptured. Dr. H. Blank heard this event from his research laboratory, then remarked on the sound. Associate researcher Dr. R. Wirth exited to investigate.

Much shorter, much quicker, much punchier. There's a number of points in this article where I feel like you use 10 words when you could use 5. And this is important, 'cuz it's a bit wordy — and you want this to feel as much like an industrial disaster report as possible.

(If you haven't already, I recommend checking out this channel and the way that a lot of these industrial incidents are described — that being said, a lot of the language you're using sounds like it's there already — my main gripe is with some of the wording and a number of cases where you use a few too many words IMO)

(Also, the most important words in a sentence ought to either be as close to the beginning or close to the end as possible. Hence swapping 'AFF-D' with 'ruptured' — ruptured is the important bit, which leads to Dr. H. Blank hearing the rupture.)

Wirth was pulled into AAF-D by a translucent orange tentacle and disappeared.

I'd take out 'disappeared'; it doesn't sound clinical. You want this to sound as much like a tedious business report as possible — make the impossible/supernatural sound ordinary, and it actually heightens the tension and dread.

Wirth was pulled into AAF-D by an orange translucent tentacle; both he and the appendage dematerialized.

Also, we don't know that Wirth perished here! Which means you might want to modify the containment procedure where you say these personnel must perish — possibly to say 'perish or otherwise be unaccounted for'? I feel like that kind of dilutes the strength of 'these personnel must perish', though, so — YMMV.

At 18:24 he called out to any personnel still alive within AAF-D, simultaneous to the appearance of technician Markey at the end of the airlock approach hallway.

At 18:24, he called out to all remaining personnel within AAF-D. This occurred simultaneous to technician Markey's arrival at the hallway approaching the airlock.

It sounds like Markey magically 'appears' in this hallway, otherwise. Also, 'airlock approach hallway' is a weird way to phrase 'the hallway approaching the airlock'.

For unknown reasons, upon hearing Chief Nascimbeni's voice technician Markey stopped running and turned around.

On hearing Chief Nascimbeni's voice, technician Markey ceased running and turned to look at an unidentified subject.

'technician Markey' also sounds very weird. Maybe 'Chief Technical Officer Markey', or 'Chief Technician Markey'?

A wave of steam passed over him and he collapsed, converting to coloured droplets as he fell. An inexplicable gust of air pulled these droplets deeper into the facility, from which screaming could now be heard.

A wave of steam passed through him, converting him to coloured droplets. A gust of air then pulled these droplets deeper into the facility.

Yeah, this is where things start to get increasingly clunky for me. A few random points:

  • 'inexplicable gust of air'? Is anything going on 'explicable', right now? Was the orange tentacle 'explicable'? — etc.
  • A lot of this is starting to feel like you're trying to write a novel instead of a technical report. Nothing against novels, and I think your descriptions are pretty good! But the aim of an SCP article is to describe the horrifying in technical terms. Remember, I shouldn't feel like you're trying to be scary. I shouldn't feel like you're trying to be cinematic. I should feel like you're just telling me precisely what happened.
  • The 'from which screaming could now be heard' is very much the move a novel would make… but again, this is not a novel. I'd cut it unless you can find a way to write this that makes it "sound" very trivial (did the screams just start? Where are they coming from? What or who is picking them up — a camera? A person?)

In fact, a thought I just had: I think this would feel more clinical and horrifying if you could find a way to present it as more of a 'log of events' rather than this sort of descriptive account. Similar, though not identical, to what you had in the containment procedures. Imagine something like this:

[18:23:05] Chief N. Nascimbeni is automatically paged by the alarm system. He proceeds to the airlock at AAF-D alongside researcher Wirth.
[18:23:45] Chief N. Nascimbeni and researcher Wirth arrive at the airlock. A translucent orange tentacle seizes Wirth, retracts into AAF-D, then dematerializes with the researcher.
[18:24:10] Technician Markey arrives in the hallway that approaches the AAF-D airlock.
[18:24:42] Chief N. Nascimbeni calls out to any remaining personnel within AAF-D.
[18:24:43] Technician Markey stops running and turns to face an unidentified subject.
[18:24:45] A wave of steam passes through technician Markey, converting him into coloured droplets. These droplets are carried deeper into AAF-D.
[18:24:48] Screams can be heard from several points throughout AAF-D.

See how this lets me throw the screaming in there as just a random detail, instead of trying to make it feel linked to Markey somehow? This is just an example of how you might do something like this, though — the point is to create a sense of detachment from the horror (which, counter-intuitively, heightens the sense of horror — by making it feel more real and less 'cinematic' — like it's not designed to scare us, but something that's just happening).

(You could also use this approach to show us several events in different areas occurring simultaneously — creating effectively several different 'plot-threads' associated with the fates of various personnel. As we flip between them, our sense of horror grows as their situations become increasingly dire — but because of the nature of the presentation, it doesn't feel like you're trying to horrify us.)

I'm not going to go through this whole thing like this, so I'll just throw out a few more random asides:

Technician Ambrogi's inside-out corpse

Given the lovely phrase of 'biologically inverted' from earlier, I feel like this is a missed opportunity. 'inside-out corpse' sounds boring: "Inverted corpse" sounds way creepier (and evocative).

Five hundred and seventy-two life-sized, photorealistic, two-dimensional representations of agent Mukami frozen in a state of extreme distress

This is fantastic, but I'd cut 'extreme' and 'frozen' (just 'agent Mukami in a state of distress') is more than enough).

An inescapable sense of "presence" in a third-floor washroom facility experienced by all searching personnel, which dissipated after twenty-four hours

'inescapable' is a cinematic word. "Reports of a presence in the third-floor washroom facility by all searching personnel." is way more clinical and creepier, imo (don't need to mention it passed after 24 hours, either; just that it was reported by searching personnel — which implies it was temporary).

But, yeah — I really like this piece, but I also feel like this piece could have way more polish and feel way more 'real'.

One more thought: Your 'stinger' here is that the anomaly 'grows' to include any new deaths that must then be included in the containment of it. You can actually hide this from us by the very end (without cheating) with just some careful phrasings.

I feel like I've already written way too much feedback on this piece, but there's still way more stuff I could touch on. Either way, I think this has a lot of great moves, and — even though I think it's a bit clunky in spots and could be way leaner, meaner, and polished — the energy of it (an anomalous temporal industrial disaster that has the potential of getting bigger as we continue to make sure everything happens precisely as it did) is great, and enough to win me over despite the fact that it isn't precisely what I think it should be.

Good job, and good luck with the article!

item.title: Re: SCP-5243: +1
item.content:encoded: That's exactly what I wanted, so you've helped make my day. I'm glad you liked it, and thanks for the very groutifying gratifying response! And the hilarious summary with the hilarious neologism.

item.title: SCP-5243: +1
item.content:encoded: An amazing piece that delivers a bizarre story with quite a lot of "lolrandom" but still manages to maintain thorough clinical tone and properly describe the anomaly at hand. No part actually felt bad or out-of-place in this, which is certainly hard to not do when people are getting turned into pseudo-lemons or beginning groutification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, and I like what you did with it too! Deeply appreciate the much-needed assistance, and all the time it must have taken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Complexity that pays off by its end. I like what you've done with the place :)

item.title: SCP-5243: "The Breach that Keeps On Breaching"!
item.content:encoded:
Remember, remember, the 8th of September! If you don't, it'll turn you into lemons.

Many hours, many thousands of pruned words, and probably a few tears went into this one. Oh, and two weeks of my life, on and off, but who's counting?

Thanks to Flopmind and caspian2 for their critiques! Boos to me if I didn't learn enough from them.

+ Image Credits
 Close

Apologies in advance to the licensing team. This is gonna take a while. (Unless this gets deleted!)

Update: Sincere thanks and even more apologies to the licensing team; the majority of my images were not, as I thought they were, CC-compliant. I have replaced them with new images which are!

Most of these images are replacements because the source of the originals decided to suck. Images that need CC verification are indicated in blue. Everything else has already been verified.

"AAFD-1.jpg" is a composite. Its components were borrowed from:
